title: Project Overview
description: Start with the README to understand what PoPunkouterSoftware is: a .NET 10 Blazor Web App built from a server host (ASP.NET Core API + Blazor shell), a WebAssembly client, a Shared contracts project, and an Infrastructure project of Azure adapters. It introduces the operational endpoints for Azure, diagnostics, GitHub activity, and infrastructure scanning, with health at /health and diagnostics at /diag, all running locally on HTTP port 8000. Read alongside ARCHITECTURE.md to see how the four projects layer together before diving into code.

title: Server Host Entry Point
description: Program.cs is the ASP.NET Core host entry point and the most depended-upon file in the project. It bootstraps Serilog logging, wires Azure Key Vault and OpenTelemetry, registers SignalR, and maps the application's minimal-API endpoint groups. This file is the runtime spine that every host feature hangs off of, so understanding its startup order frames everything that follows.
languageLesson: ASP.NET Core minimal APIs use a top-level WebApplicationBuilder/WebApplication pair: services are registered on builder.Services, then endpoints are mapped via extension methods like app.MapXxxEndpoints(), keeping each feature's routes in its own file.

title: Health & Diagnostics Endpoints
description: Building on the host wired up in Step 2, these endpoint groups expose the operational surface promised by the README. HealthEndpoints maps /health and the client-config endpoint reporting application and dependency status, while DiagEndpoints serves the Azure efficiency report and triggers and cancels report refreshes under /api/diag/*. Together they are the diagnostic front door used by both the dashboard and the test suite.

title: Operational Endpoint Groups
description: Beyond diagnostics, the host exposes feature-specific endpoint groups that round out the operational API. PingerEndpoints surfaces the background service-ping snapshot and a toggle, GitHubEndpoints fetches repository activity and computes a health score, and InfraEndpoints scans GitHub repositories for workflow and IaC files. Each follows the same minimal-API mapping pattern from Step 3, keeping a clean per-feature folder structure.

title: SignalR Refresh Hub
description: Long-running Azure report refreshes need live progress updates, and that is the job of RefreshHub, a SignalR hub that broadcasts refresh progress and completion events to connected clients. RefreshSessionManager is the singleton that coordinates a single concurrent refresh run using a lock and a cancellation token, ensuring the diag endpoints from Step 3 never start two scans at once. This real-time channel is what keeps the client dashboard in Step 8 live.
languageLesson: A SignalR Hub is a server-side class whose public methods clients can call and which can push messages back to clients over a persistent WebSocket connection, enabling server-initiated progress events rather than client polling.

title: Azure Report Orchestration
description: The Infrastructure project holds the Azure adapters that do the real work behind the endpoints. AzureReportService is the core orchestrator that discovers web services and builds the full inventory report, AzureReportStore persists reports to Azure Table Storage with gzip compression, and ServicePingerService is the background hosted service that periodically pings service URLs to record up/down status. This is where the diagnostics surface from Steps 3-5 gets its data.

title: Diagnosis & Recommendations
description: On top of the raw inventory, the Infrastructure project layers analysis services that turn data into insight. DowntimeDiagnosisService correlates ARM deployments and activity logs to explain why a service is down, IncidentService detects status transitions between report runs and records incidents, and PlanRecommendationService produces prioritized App Service Plan recommendations from config drift and downtime diagnoses. These services feed the operational and portfolio views the dashboard renders later.

title: Shared Contracts & Models
description: The Shared project is the contract layer that both the host and the WASM client compile against, so the same types cross the wire without duplication. AzureModels defines the record/DTO definitions for the entire Azure report domain, while Result<T> is the generic success/failure type used throughout the codebase for explicit error handling. Understanding these records makes the dashboard code in Step 9 immediately readable.
languageLesson: C# record types give value-based equality and concise immutable DTOs, ideal for serialized contracts shared between server and WebAssembly client. A generic Result<T> wraps success/failure plus a value or error, making failure paths explicit instead of relying on exceptions.

title: Client Entry & Landing Page
description: Now switch to the browser side. The Client Program.cs is the Blazor WebAssembly entry point that configures the root components, the typed HttpClient pointing at the host, and client services. Index.razor is the landing/portfolio page that fetches the live Azure app inventory from the endpoints in Step 4 and renders it as a hero portfolio view, your first taste of the data flowing end to end.
languageLesson: Blazor WebAssembly runs C# in the browser via WebAssembly. The client Program.cs uses WebAssemblyHostBuilder to register root components and inject an HttpClient whose BaseAddress targets the host, so Razor pages can call the server's minimal APIs directly.

title: Azure Operations Dashboard
description: The Azure dashboard is the application's centerpiece UI, split across a markup file and several partial-class code files for maintainability. AzureDashboard.razor renders fleet health, charts, and the portfolio table; AzureDashboard.razor.cs is the primary code-behind that loads reports and handles SignalR-driven refresh updates from the hub in Step 5; and the Operations partial computes operational health and cost-watch views from the shared models in Step 8. This is where every earlier layer converges into a live operator view.
languageLesson: C# partial classes let one logical Blazor component span several files (.razor markup plus multiple .cs files), so a large page like the dashboard can separate concerns — charts, operations, portfolio, trends — without one giant file.

title: Automated Testing
description: The project is verified at three levels. Integration tests boot the real host via a WebApplicationFactory fixture (TestWebAppFixture) and ApiEndpointTests exercises the health, config, diag, and OpenAPI endpoints from Steps 3-4 in-process. The Playwright pages.spec.ts E2E spec then drives a real browser to confirm the Home, Azure Dashboard, and Diag pages load, hydrate the Blazor WASM client, and render without JS errors, validating the full stack you have just toured.
languageLesson: WebApplicationFactory<T> from ASP.NET Core spins up the app in-memory for integration tests, giving real HTTP behavior without a network. Playwright complements this with browser-level E2E tests that assert pages render and hydrate correctly.

title: Build & Deployment Pipeline
description: Finally, the capstone: how this all ships. The GitHub Actions deploy.yml workflow builds and deploys the .NET 10 Blazor app to Azure App Service, while docker-compose.yml describes the containerized local/service composition. Together they close the loop from the source you have toured to a running deployment, mirroring the production target the Azure dashboard monitors.
languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows are YAML files triggered by 'on' events, with 'jobs' running 'steps' in sequence; here the pipeline restores, builds, tests, and deploys to Azure. Docker Compose declares multi-service stacks so the app and its dependencies start together with one command.
